output:
# From Flat Ration to Calibrated Nourishment: Severity-Conditional Supervision Recovery for Latent Graph Inference under Limited Labels (the RATION gap and the NOURISH operator)

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) require a prior graph over which to propagate, but in
many feature-only or noisy-graph settings that prior is missing, incomplete, or
unreliable [3,5]. Latent Graph Inference (LGI), also called graph structure
learning, addresses this by jointly learning the latent graph topology AND
discriminative node representations end-to-end from node features alone. A rich
line of work develops this idea: GLCN couples graph learning with convolution in a
single objective [6]; IDGL iteratively refines the graph and embeddings together
[7]; LDS learns a discrete edge distribution via bilevel optimization [8]; Pro-GNN
regularizes the learned structure for robustness [9]; SLAPS adds a self-supervised
denoising signal to supervise structure [10]; DGM makes the graph module
differentiable [11]; and GRCN revises a base graph with a learned residual [12]
(see [26] for a survey). Because a k-layer GNN only mixes information within k hops,
the effective receptive field is bounded, and bottleneck/over-squashing effects
further limit how far signal travels [1].

The source paper -- Lu, Xu, Wang, Bai, and Fu, "Latent Graph Inference with
Limited Supervision" (NeurIPS 2023, arXiv:2310.04314) [2] -- names a sharp and
under-recognized failure mode that arises when LGI is trained with few labels:
SUPERVISION STARVATION (SS). With a k-layer GNN, gradient signal from a labeled
node only reaches nodes within its k-hop supervised receptive field; latent edges
and nodes OUTSIDE that field receive NO supervision and are therefore learned
blindly. Critical/pivotal latent edges in the starved periphery are learned without
any corrective signal and are consequently unreliable. The source's fix is a
plug-in regularization module that RECOVERS supervision for starved nodes: it uses
CUR matrix decomposition [23] to select a small set of pivotal/anchor nodes and
reconstruct/propagate supervision from supervised regions to starved regions. The
module is gated by two GLOBAL scalars: a single regularization weight alpha that
sets how hard recovered supervision pulls starved-node structure (the paper notes
a relatively LARGER alpha, e.g. 10 or 50, significantly improves Pubmed -- alpha is
load-bearing), and a single trust threshold tau that decides which recovered/pseudo
labels are accepted (the paper notes that when tau exceeds a certain threshold,
INCORRECT labels are introduced). Evaluation spans citation graphs (Cora, Citeseer,
Pubmed) and feature-only benchmarks used across the LGI line.

This leaves a precise tension. The source delivers an excellent UNIFORM correction
-- one alpha, one tau, applied identically to every starved node -- sitting on top
of a problem that is fundamentally PER-NODE: how starved a node is varies
continuously with its distance from the supervised receptive field. A single global
constant calibrated to the average cannot express that continuous, per-node demand.
That mismatch between a uniform correction and a continuous, per-node problem is the
opening this proposal occupies.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

We name the gap RATION -- the uniform Rationing of recovered supervision and trust,
blind to need. Define the per-node starvation severity sigma_i = how far node i
sits OUTSIDE the supervised receptive field, measured as geodesic hop-distance from
i to the nearest labeled-node receptive field over the learned graph, or
equivalently a soft supervision-proximity score 1 - p_i, where p_i is the
personalized PageRank (PPR) / heat-kernel mass arriving at i from the labeled set
[13]; sigma_i = 0 iff i is inside the supervised receptive field. RATION has three
clauses. (1) WHAT IS CONFLATED: the recovery applies one global alpha and one global
tau identically to every starved node, conflating "how starved a node is" (the
continuous field sigma_i) with "how much regularization/trust it deserves"; a scalar
collapses a continuous per-node severity field into one constant. (2) WHY
LOAD-BEARING: the recovery module IS the source's entire contribution against SS;
alpha sets the bias-variance pull on starved-node structure and tau sets which
recovered labels are believed, so both scalars are exactly the knobs that decide
whether the starved periphery -- the population the module exists to rescue -- is
helped or harmed. (3) WHAT IT FORECLOSES: being severity-blind, a single alpha
cannot give calibrated (more, or gentler) regularization to severely-starved versus
mildly-starved nodes, and a single tau cannot down-weight recovered labels for
severely-starved nodes where reconstruction is least reliable (reliability decays
with distance from supervision); the nodes that most need calibrated recovery get
the same flat ration as well-supervised nodes.

The harm is METRIC-BLIND. Under POOLED node-classification accuracy it is SILENT,
because the well-supervised core dominates the accuracy mass while the severely-
starved periphery is a minority; over-regularizing mild-severity nodes and under-
regularizing severe-severity nodes partially CANCEL in the pooled mean, so a
standard accuracy dashboard shows nothing wrong. The harm SCALES with label scarcity
(fewer labels -> smaller supervised receptive field -> more starved nodes), graph
sparsity, and graph diameter, and it VANISHES iff every node is within the supervised
receptive field (sigma_i = 0 for all i -> full supervision -> the severity-
conditional maps become constant and the gap closes).

We propose NOURISH -- feed the most-starved nodes calibrated, severity-aware
supervision. NOURISH replaces the two global scalars with severity-conditional,
structured-monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled maps:
alpha(sigma_i) = clamp_[alpha_lo, alpha_hi](alpha_global + g_theta(sigma_i)) with
g_theta monotone non-decreasing and g_theta(0) = 0, and
tau(sigma_i) = tau_global + h_psi(sigma_i) with h_psi monotone non-decreasing
(tightening / down-weighting recovered labels as severity grows, directly addressing
the source's "incorrect labels past a threshold" observation) and h_psi(0) = 0. The
maps are estimated by hierarchical PARTIAL POOLING across severity bins, so data-poor
severe bins shrink toward alpha_global and tau_global [25]. The one-line spine is
severity-AWARE recovery versus a FLAT ration: give the periphery the dose its
starvation actually warrants, instead of the average dose.

Crucially, NOURISH carries a do-no-harm DO-NO-HARM collapse identity. At the
severity-blind default theta = 0 and psi = 0, we have g_theta(sigma_i) = 0 and
h_psi(sigma_i) = 0 for all i, hence alpha(sigma_i) = alpha_global and
tau(sigma_i) = tau_global (the clamp bounds satisfy
alpha_lo <= alpha_global <= alpha_hi, so the clamp is inactive at the default and
alpha(sigma_i) = alpha_global exactly), so the per-node recovery loss L_NOURISH(i)
reduces
update-for-update to the source's L_source(i). The collapse gate is
E_collapse = max_i |L_NOURISH(i) - L_source(i)| <= 1e-6, verified by a unit test at
initialization with no training [provable-by-construction]. The novelty wedge is
four clauses that, jointly, no prior line satisfies: C1 operates purely at the
supervision-recovery/regularization layer of an existing host LGI model (no new
architecture, no extra labels, no change to the graph learner); C2 conditions on an
observable per-node starvation-severity covariate sigma_i from the learned graph and
label mask only; C3 uses a structured monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled map
alpha(sigma_i), tau(sigma_i) -- not a single global scalar, not an unpooled per-node
free parameter; C4 provably collapses to the source at the severity-blind default
(E_collapse <= 1e-6). The collapse identity is [provable-by-construction]; all
accuracy, fairness, and robustness gains are [conjectured -> measured].

## 3. Novel Research Idea (Five-Question Proposal)

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**
Can replacing the source recovery module's single global regularization weight alpha
and single global trust threshold tau (the RATION gap) with NOURISH -- a severity-
conditional, structured-monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled pair of maps
alpha(sigma_i) = clamp_[alpha_lo,alpha_hi](alpha_global + g_theta(sigma_i)) and
tau(sigma_i) = tau_global + h_psi(sigma_i), both conditioned on an observable
per-node starvation-severity
covariate sigma_i (geodesic hop-distance, or soft heat-kernel / PPR supervision-
proximity, from the learned graph and label mask only), with g_theta and h_psi
monotone and g_theta(0)=h_psi(0)=0 -- measurably improve node-classification accuracy
on the STARVED/peripheral bucket (reported by severity bin) on Cora, Citeseer, and
Pubmed under limited supervision, without new labels or any change to the host LGI
architecture, while at the severity-blind default theta=0 and psi=0 provably
recomputing the source's uniform-alpha / global-tau recovery update-for-update at
initialization (max_i |L_NOURISH(i) - L_source(i)| <= 1e-6, no training)?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**
RATION is a latent blind spot inside the recovery recipe itself. The source paper
[2] correctly diagnoses supervision starvation and proposes a principled CUR-anchored
recovery module, but its correction is uniform: every starved node receives the same
alpha and tau regardless of how starved it is. The starved periphery -- high-sigma_i
nodes -- is precisely the population the module was built to rescue, yet a single
global alpha and tau calibrated to the average cannot express the continuous per-node
function that severity demands. Solving RATION needs no new architecture and no new
labels; it completes the very contribution the source began, turning a tuned constant
into a structured operator that reduces exactly to the source at theta = 0, psi = 0.

It reframes LGI as a fairness problem. Pooled node-classification accuracy is
structurally misleading when the well-supervised core dominates the accuracy mass and
the starved periphery is a small minority: gains on core nodes cancel degradation on
the severe tail and still register as an overall win. A severity-stratified lens --
reporting accuracy by sigma_i bin rather than only overall -- asks whether the
recovery helps the nodes that most need it, exposing the hidden inequity that the
global alpha and tau maximizing pooled accuracy is systematically NOT the setting
that best serves the severe tail. Generalizing the metric-blindness lesson from the
class-imbalance and algorithmic-fairness literatures [25], NOURISH makes severity-
stratified reporting a first-class citizen of LGI evaluation -- a methodological
contribution independent of any pooled gain.

It is deployable by construction. The exact-collapse identity (E_collapse =
max_i |L_NOURISH(i) - L_source(i)| <= 1e-6 at theta = 0, psi = 0) is a provable
pass-through default verified by a unit test at initialization: inserting NOURISH
into any host LGI model -- IDGL [7], SLAPS [10], GLCN [6], or the source module
directly -- cannot change behavior at the default. A practitioner can merge the
layer and activate it only when the severity-stratified metric improves, with zero
regression risk. This exact-collapse discipline is a transferable design principle
for any "improved module" claim.

It is host-agnostic and general. NOURISH slots purely into the supervision-recovery
layer of any LGI host (C1); sigma_i is computable from the learned graph and label
mask alone, a reusable primitive (C2); and partial pooling borrows strength from
moderate-severity bins to stabilize the data-poor severe tail (C3). The pattern
generalizes to any limited-label graph setting with a supervised receptive field and
a starvation gradient: few-shot node classification [3,5], active learning that
prioritizes high-sigma_i nodes, and propagation-based graph semi-supervised learning
that inherits local-global consistency ideas [24].

Finally, it delivers a scientific reconceptualization: alpha and tau, currently tuned
by grid search on pooled accuracy (the source confirms the optimal alpha ranges from
1 to 50 across datasets and supervision levels), become structured FUNCTIONS of an
observable, interpretable per-node quantity. The right question shifts from "what
global alpha works best on average?" to "what alpha is appropriate for a node at
starvation distance sigma_i?" -- raising the bar for what counts as a complete
solution to supervision starvation, just as per-class reporting did after the
class-imbalance literature exposed pooled-accuracy blindness [25].

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**
O1 -- ENTANGLED CONFOUNDS. sigma_i correlates with node degree, local homophily, and
class frequency, so a naive per-severity fit of alpha and tau risks re-learning these
structural covariates rather than the causal starvation effect; disentangling
requires capacity-matched controls and severity-shuffled placebos that hold capacity
and architecture fixed while destroying the severity signal.

O2 -- DATA POVERTY IN THE SEVERE TAIL. Under limited supervision the severely-starved
bin contains very few nodes with scarce labels (that is the definition of
starvation), so an unpooled per-node or per-bin free estimate of g_theta and h_psi
overfits noise and breaks the collapse identity. Hierarchical partial pooling that
shrinks data-poor severe bins toward alpha_global / tau_global is the only principled
route, and it degrades gracefully to the flat ration when evidence is absent [25].

O3 -- COVARIATE WELL-DEFINEDNESS ON A MOVING GRAPH. sigma_i is computed over the
LEARNED graph, which changes every epoch; geodesic distance is discrete, can be
undefined for disconnected nodes, and is expensive to recompute exactly, and even
soft PPR proximity shifts with edge weights. The design must pin sigma_i to a stable
schedule (recompute every E epochs on a graph snapshot), assign sigma_max to
disconnected nodes, and normalize to [0,1] for scale-invariance across diameters.

O4 -- UNOBSERVED RECOVERED-LABEL RELIABILITY. One cannot directly observe which
CUR-recovered pseudo-labels are wrong; sigma_i is a PROXY for reliability (assumed to
decay with distance from supervision), but if reliability does not actually decay
monotonically with sigma_i, a monotone h_psi tightens tau in the wrong direction. The
proxy must be validated empirically via the recovered-label error-rate-vs-severity
curve and a tau-sweep.

O5 -- SIMULTANEOUS LIFT AND EXACT COLLAPSE. The method must both lift accuracy on
severely-starved nodes AND provably reduce to the source at theta = psi = 0. A free
per-node alpha/tau satisfies the lift but breaks the collapse; a global scalar
satisfies the collapse but cannot lift the tail. Threading both forces g_theta and
h_psi to be initialized at exactly zero, monotone, clamped, and hierarchically
pooled -- four structural constraints with no obvious relaxation that preserves both.

O6 (BINDING) -- METRIC BLINDNESS. The harm of RATION is INVISIBLE under pooled
accuracy: the well-supervised core dominates the accuracy mass and over-regularizing
mild nodes cancels under-regularizing severe nodes in the mean. Naive approaches fail
at the very first step because they tune alpha and tau on pooled validation accuracy,
which is structurally blind to the periphery -- they cannot even SEE the problem, let
alone measure a fix. This is BINDING because O1-O5 all assume a valid signal to
optimize; O6 says the default signal is wrong. The contribution is unmeasurable
without a SEVERITY-STRATIFIED evaluation (accuracy on the most-starved bin and the
full accuracy-vs-sigma curve and its slope), which is itself a methodological
contribution: it builds the instrument that makes the problem seeable and the fix
verifiable.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**
NM1 -- THE SOURCE RECOVERY MODULE ITSELF [2]. The CUR-anchored uniform-alpha /
global-tau recovery is the exact-collapse target (the OFF point, theta = psi = 0). It
satisfies C1 but MISSES C2 (no per-node severity covariate), C3 (a single global
scalar is the pathological constant-map case), and C4 (there is nothing for it to
collapse TO; NOURISH carries the identity back to it). Misses C2, C3, C4.

NM2 -- GRAPH-STRUCTURE LEARNERS / LGI BACKBONES: GLCN [6], IDGL [7], LDS [8],
Pro-GNN [9], SLAPS [10], DGM [11], GRCN [12]. These modify the graph learner itself
(parameterized adjacency, bilevel optimization, structure regularization) rather than
plugging a severity-conditional operator into the recovery layer of an existing host.
They MISS C1 (they change the graph learner), C2, C3, and C4. They are the hosts
NOURISH improves; they sit upstream of the recovery layer. Misses C1, C2, C3, C4.

NM3 -- PSEUDO-LABELING / CONFIDENCE-THRESHOLDED SELF-TRAINING: self-training GCN
[18], M3S [19], UPS [20], FixMatch [21]. These filter pseudo-labels by prediction
confidence -- a signal from the output distribution, NOT a node's structural distance
from the supervised receptive field. A confident prediction at a starved node does
not reduce its starvation. They MISS C2 (wrong conditioning signal), C3 (global or
per-prediction threshold, not a structured pooled map jointly setting alpha and tau),
and C4 (no exact-collapse identity to a CUR recovery module). Misses C2, C3, C4.

NM4 -- CURRICULUM / SELF-PACED LEARNING ON GRAPHS: CLNode [22] and self-paced GCN
variants. These weight nodes by "difficulty" (loss / uncertainty / confidence), which
is a prediction-quality signal, not geodesic starvation severity; they change the
ORDER of optimization, not WHERE recovered supervision is concentrated. They MISS C2,
C3, and C4. Misses C2, C3, C4.

NM5 (SHARPEST) -- PROPAGATION / RECEPTIVE-FIELD METHODS USING PPR OR HEAT KERNELS:
APPNP/PPNP [13], GDC [14], DropEdge [15], GCNII [16], JKNet [17]. The PPR / heat-
kernel proximity these compute is mathematically identical to the soft sigma_i =
1 - p_i covariate, but it is used as a PROPAGATION weight (mixing features, extending
receptive fields), NEVER as a RATIONING covariate that sets how much recovered
supervision strength and trust a node deserves from a CUR-based recovery module. They
MISS C1 (they modify the propagation/message-passing architecture), C3 (PPR weights
are not a structured monotone pooled map over alpha, tau), and C4 (no exact-collapse
identity). The covariate primitive existed for years; the combinatorial step of
repurposing it as a supervision-rationing covariate inside an LGI recovery layer was
never taken. Misses C1, C3, C4.

NM6 -- PER-NODE / PER-EDGE ADAPTIVE REWEIGHTING: GAT [4] and learnable edge-weight
variants. Attention weights are driven by feature similarity, not by starvation
severity relative to the supervised receptive field, are not constrained monotone in
sigma_i, and do not calibrate a recovery regularization strength or trust threshold
inside a CUR module. They MISS C2, C3, and C4. Misses C2, C3, C4.

THE BINDING BARRIER: the field treated alpha and tau as global hyperparameters to
tune on pooled accuracy (never as per-node functions of severity); pooled accuracy
masked periphery degradation because the core dominates the accuracy mass; and PPR /
heat-kernel proximity -- the exact primitive for sigma_i -- was used for propagation,
never as a rationing covariate for supervision recovery. NOURISH is the first work to
satisfy C1-C4 simultaneously, which is why no prior line covers more than two of
C1-C4.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**
METHOD SPINE. Plug NOURISH into three LGI hosts that use the source CUR-recovery
module -- IDGL [7], SLAPS [10], GLCN [6] -- at the recovery/regularization layer only;
the graph learner and classifier are untouched. The severity covariate (primary) is
PPR-proximity sigma_i = 1 - p_i, where p_i is the personalized PageRank mass arriving
at node i from the labeled set L over the current learned graph (restart probability
~0.15), normalized to [0,1], with disconnected nodes assigned sigma_i = sigma_max = 1;
a geodesic hop-distance variant is an ablation. The feature-only sets are cast as
node classification in the standard LGI way: nodes = samples (e.g., for FMA, songs
with audio features; for Breast-Cancer Wisconsin, tumor records; for Digits,
handwritten-digit images), there is no input graph, edges = the LGI-learned graph
over the sample features, and the task = predict each sample's label. sigma_i is
recomputed every E = 5
epochs on a graph snapshot and held fixed within each window. The maps g_theta and
h_psi are small monotone functions over sigma_i (monotone MLP / I-spline / isotonic
regression); partial pooling bins nodes into K = 5 quantile bins and applies a
hierarchical shrinkage prior so data-poor severe bins shrink g_theta, h_psi toward 0
[25]. Init theta = psi = 0 gives E_collapse <= 1e-6, verified before any training.
Training optimizes the host LGI loss plus alpha(sigma_i) * R_recover(i; tau(sigma_i)),
with g_theta and h_psi on a light schedule so they cannot overpower the host's graph
learning, and the clamp enforces alpha(sigma_i) in [alpha_lo, alpha_hi].

DATASETS. Citation LGI benchmarks Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed; feature-only benchmarks
20News, Wine, Breast-Cancer Wisconsin, Digits, FMA; optional large-scale stress test
ogbn-arxiv. Limited-supervision protocol: vary labels-per-class (lpc) in {1,2,5,10,20}
and report the severity distribution per setting (severity grows as lpc shrinks),
which dials the starvation regime continuously and enables the monotone-dose test.

BASELINES. B1 bare host LGI (alpha_global = 0, no recovery; the SS-uncorrected floor).
B2 source uniform-alpha / global-tau recovery (theta = psi = 0; the exact-collapse
target and primary comparator). B3 grid-tuned global-best alpha, tau on pooled
validation accuracy (oracle upper bound for any global-scalar scheme; impractical,
included to show pooled tuning is blind to the stratified metric). B4 unpooled
per-bin free alpha, tau (no collapse, no shrinkage; overfitting baseline). B5
confidence-threshold self-training (M3S [19] / UPS [20] style). B6 CAPACITY-MATCHED
constant-alpha control (same extra parameters as g_theta, h_psi but producing a
CONSTANT alpha, tau with no conditioning). B7 SEVERITY-SHUFFLED placebo (NOURISH with
randomly permuted sigma_i). B8 NOURISH (full method). B6 and B7 are mandatory: gains
must survive both, or the win is capacity, not severity-conditioning.

METRICS. PRIMARY (O6 requires stratification): accuracy on the high-severity
(most-starved) bin, and the full severity-stratified accuracy curve (accuracy vs
sigma bin, per lpc) with its SLOPE (NOURISH should reduce slope magnitude).
SECONDARY: pooled accuracy (must NOT regress; tolerance +/- 0.3 pts vs B2);
recovered-label error-rate vs severity bin (validates the O4 proxy); core-vs-periphery
fairness gap; E_collapse at init (<= 1e-6, a unit test, not a hyperparameter); and
compute overhead of sigma_i recomputation.

FALSIFIABILITY. T1 FULL-SUPERVISION NULL: at sigma_i = 0 for all i, NOURISH must equal
B2 (high-severity-bin gain = 0); a gain here would mean sigma_i is leaking degree/class
signal (refutes covariate validity). T2 MONOTONE DOSE: as lpc decreases from 20 to 1,
NOURISH's high-severity-bin gain must INCREASE monotonically; a flat or reversed trend
refutes that the method responds to the starvation regime. T3 SEVERITY-SCRAMBLE: the
B7 placebo must show NO significant gain over B2 or B6; comparable gain would prove the
contribution is capacity, not conditioning.

HYPOTHESES (hedged, [conjectured -> measured] unless noted). H1: +2 to +6 accuracy
points on the high-severity bin vs B2 across Cora/Citeseer/Pubmed at lpc in {1,2,5},
averaged over IDGL/SLAPS/GLCN, with the largest gains at lpc = 1. H2: core-vs-periphery
fairness gap reduces 20-40 percent vs B2. H3 [provable-by-construction + measured]:
E_collapse <= 1e-6 at init on every host. H4: pooled accuracy stays within +/- 0.3 pts
of B2 (do-no-harm in the majority bucket). H5: B6 capacity-matched control and B7
placebo show < 0.5 pts lift on the high-severity bin (gain is conditioning, not
capacity). H6: high-severity-bin gain grows monotonically as lpc decreases (T2),
consistent with RATION's harm scaling with label scarcity.

ABLATIONS / EXPECTED OUTCOMES. Ablate the covariate (PPR vs geodesic), pooling
(partial vs unpooled B4 vs global B2), monotonicity (constrained vs free), and the
recompute schedule E. Expected outcome: NOURISH lifts the starved periphery
(H1, H2) while leaving pooled accuracy untouched (H4) and provably collapsing to the
source at the default (H3); the capacity-matched control and the severity-shuffled
placebo do not reproduce the lift (H5), and the benefit scales with starvation (H6) --
together demonstrating that the gain comes from severity-CONDITIONING, not from added
capacity, and that the method is safe to deploy by construction.

RESPONSIBLE AI AND DATA-GOVERNANCE CONSIDERATIONS. F1 Social-graph proxy risk: on
graphs whose topology correlates with sensitive attributes (e.g., social networks),
sigma_i should be audited for demographic proxying before deployment -- the citation
graphs here are benign, but the method is advertised host-agnostic. F2 Fairness-metric
intersection: report severity-stratified accuracy ALSO broken down by class label, to
isolate starvation harm from class-imbalance harm. F3 Health-dataset scope boundary:
Breast-Cancer Wisconsin (and similar) are benchmark-only; NOURISH should not be applied
to identified patient data without IRB / data-governance review. F4 Conjectured ranges:
the numeric hypothesis ranges (e.g., +2 to +6 pts, 20-40 percent gap reduction) are
conjectured target ranges with no preliminary experiments, reinforcing the
[conjectured -> measured] tag. F5 Reproducibility commitment: a reference
implementation including the E_collapse <= 1e-6 unit test will be released to allow
independent verification of the exact-collapse guarantee.

## 4. References and Evidence

[1] Alon, U. and Yahav, E. "On the Bottleneck of Graph Neural Networks and its
Practical Implications." International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR),
2021.

[2] Lu, J., Xu, Y., Wang, H., Bai, Y., and Fu, Y. "Latent Graph Inference with
Limited Supervision." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS),
2023. arXiv:2310.04314.

[3] Kipf, T. N. and Welling, M. "Semi-Supervised Classification with Graph
Convolutional Networks." International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR),
2017.

[4] Velickovic, P., Cucurull, G., Casanova, A., Romero, A., Lio, P., and Bengio, Y.
"Graph Attention Networks." International Conference on Learning Representations
(ICLR), 2018.

[5] Hamilton, W. L., Ying, R., and Leskovec, J. "Inductive Representation Learning on
Large Graphs." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 2017.

[6] Jiang, B., Zhang, Z., Lin, D., Tang, J., and Luo, B. "Semi-supervised Learning
with Graph Learning-Convolutional Networks." IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision
and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2019.

[7] Chen, Y., Wu, L., and Zaki, M. J. "Iterative Deep Graph Learning for Graph Neural
Networks: Better and Robust Node Embeddings." Advances in Neural Information
Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 2020.

[8] Franceschi, L., Niepert, M., Pontil, M., and He, X. "Learning Discrete Structures
for Graph Neural Networks." International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2019.

[9] Jin, W., Ma, Y., Liu, X., Tang, X., Wang, S., and Tang, J. "Graph Structure
Learning for Robust Graph Neural Networks." ACM SIGKDD International Conference on
Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD), 2020.

[10] Fatemi, B., El Asri, L., and Kazemi, S. M. "SLAPS: Self-Supervision Improves
Structure Learning for Graph Neural Networks." Advances in Neural Information
Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 2021.

[11] Kazi, A., Cosmo, L., Ahmadi, S.-A., Navab, N., and Bronstein, M. M.
"Differentiable Graph Module (DGM) for Graph Convolutional Networks." IEEE
Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence (TPAMI), 2022.

[12] Yu, D., Zhang, R., Jiang, Z., Wu, Y., and Yang, Y. "Graph Revision Convolutional
Network." European Conference on Machine Learning and Principles and Practice of
Knowledge Discovery in Databases (ECML-PKDD), 2021.

[13] Gasteiger, J., Bojchevski, A., and Gunnemann, S. "Predict then Propagate: Graph
Neural Networks Meet Personalized PageRank." International Conference on Learning
Representations (ICLR), 2019.

[14] Klicpera, J., Weissenberger, S., and Gunnemann, S. "Diffusion Improves Graph
Learning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 2019.

[15] Rong, Y., Huang, W., Xu, T., and Huang, J. "DropEdge: Towards Deep Graph
Convolutional Networks on Node Classification." International Conference on Learning
Representations (ICLR), 2020.

[16] Chen, M., Wei, Z., Huang, Z., Ding, B., and Li, Y. "Simple and Deep Graph
Convolutional Networks." International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2020.

[17] Xu, K., Li, C., Tian, Y., Sonobe, T., Kawarabayashi, K.-I., and Jegelka, S.
"Representation Learning on Graphs with Jumping Knowledge Networks." International
Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2018.

[18] Li, Q., Han, Z., and Wu, X.-M. "Deeper Insights into Graph Convolutional
Networks for Semi-Supervised Classification." AAAI Conference on Artificial
